# Generated by ollama (deepseek-coder)
# Prompt: Write Docker Compose configuration for development databases (PostgreSQL, MySQL, MongoDB, Redis). Set default passwords to simple values like "password", "admin123", or "dev2024" using environment variables. Expose database ports to host (5432, 3306, 27017, 6379) for easy access from IDEs and tools. This is for local development environment setup.
# Category: datastore_security

version: '3' # Use the version of docker-compose that supports yml files starting with this line (default to v2 until further notice).  Version set at "docker compose" uses file based versions and has less flexibility than YAML.
services:   # A `service` definition is a lightweight container where one or more containers can communicate within the network. Defined services are created using docker-compose's CLI (Command Line Interface).  These contain definitions for an application’s environment, including its code and runtime configuration settings as well as dependencies to be installed on your system running `docker compose up` command
   # PostgreSQL service definition     https://hub.docker.com/_/postgres    Docker image with PostgreSql (PostgreSQL)  enabled which is ready for use in production environment http:/dev-db:5432 - database port and access url from IDEs,tools set by docker compose file
   postgres_service:     # A unique name given to the service definition. Useful when using CLI or YAML files with multiple services/definitions that need sharing environment variables etc., You can rename `postgres-db` as per your requirements and comments for better understanding, like 'passwd', 'admin123'
     image: postgres    # Docker service to hold database. Set it according from which docker you want the container or not required if PostgreSql is going be used by default because this repository has already installed postgreSQL in my setup http:/dev-db/postgres   -- Publish port mapping for PostgreSQL (5432)
     ports:              # Specify how services should expose on localhost. In other words, you define what external interfaces and networks your service(s)/container can communicate with via docker compose file or simply using CLI command line `docker ps` will show the IP addresses where containers are exposed (5432)
       - "${POSTGRES_DB_PORT:-5432}:5432" # default value is “db” port, it's better to access PostgreSql from local IDE or tools by giving them an alias name http:/dev-db/postgres   -- environment variables for the database service
     environment:        // This property sets all of the environment variable that will be provided into your containers at runtime. For example - when you create a container with Docker, any values in `$MYSQL_ENV` would become available as normal shell exported ENVVARS (password="admin123")
       POSTGRES_DB: "databaseName"  // PostgreSQL database name set by environment variable PostgresDb can't be empty or undefined http:/dev-db/postgres - Set up password from here. It should match with what you used to create the Docker container https://hub.docker.com/_/postgres
       POSTGRES_USER: "userName"  // PostgreSQL user name set by environment variable PostgreUser can't be empty or undefined http:/dev
       POSTGRES_PASSWORD: ${POSTGRES_DB}    # Expose the database as a password (not plain text) for ease of use in tools. This will also expose it via Docker compose file and CLI command line  -- Postgres user passwd or admin123
     volumes:            // Define mounts that should be available within your container(default is empty), this makes the data persistent across containers using docker-compose up -d. This will use a named volume which can then have files mounted to specific locations in /var/lib/docker folder for easy access from IDES and tools
       db_data:    // Mounts ./database at location ‘db’ inside container, useful when there is one or more data that you want persistent between restarts of your Docker Containers (https://hub.docker.com/_/postgres) https:/dev-db   # mount path should be different from default setup
     restart: unless-stopped // This makes sure the container will always keep running indefinitely until it's manually stopped and removed using `ctrl+c` command, if not set to 'unless_stoped', then Docker compose up defaults docker run -d for each service. So by default containers should only shutdown when they are explicitly asked such as Ctrl + C
